answer:
**In *San Hyan Mahajnana*, the strongest single passage defining *jñāna* (knowledge/wisdom) appears in the solution to one of the text's core riddles on the illusory appearances within the body (a flower in the sky, fire in water, etc.). It equates *jñāna* directly with the holy *ātmā* (Self), such that realizing this identity liberates one from rebirth:**

> The *pradhāna* (*prakṛti*) is the night. The revered *puruṣa* is the sun that rises in the night. The holy *ātmā* is said to be *jñāna* (wisdom/knowledge). When one knows all of this, let there be no doubt that one returns to the abode of the Lord.[^1]

This working definition frames *jñāna* as the essential nature of the *ātmā*—subtle, liberating self-knowledge that transcends states of consciousness (*jāgrat*, *svapna*, *suṣupti*, *tūrya*) and reveals the indwelling Lord Śiva.[^2]

**In *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, by contrast, *jñāna* receives a more explicit, analytical definition at the outset, as a property of *cetana* (consciousness):**

> *Cetana* (consciousness) is *jñāna* (knowledge), awareness, memory of teachings, not lapsing into forgetfulness.[^3]

Here, *jñāna* is the active luminosity of consciousness (*cetana* vs. *acetana*, forgetfulness), enabling discernment of reality (*tattva*) amid the *triguṇa* and states of being. It aligns with but does not equate to the *ātmā*; instead, perfect knowledge (*samyagjñāna*) is the "lamp" for yogic practice (*prayogasandhi*) returning all to Paramaśiva.[^4]

**Comparison:** The definitions differ. *Mahajnana*'s is mystical and identificatory (*jñāna* = *ātmā*-Śiva), emphasizing direct realization for *mokṣa*. *Tattvajnana*'s is systematic and functional (*jñāna* as conscious awareness/memory), a tool within a cosmogonic hierarchy of *tattva*s. They overlap in tying knowledge to subtle consciousness but are not identical—*Mahajnana* is poetic/esoteric, *Tattvajnana* taxonomic/practical.[^5]

[^1]: *San Hyan Mahajnana*, ch. 1, [para.](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/translation/#p-c60edd).
[^2]: *San Hyan Mahajnana*, ch. 1, [paras.](/works/unknown-san-hyan-mahajnana-11c531/01/translation/#p-1ce441-p-c60edd).
[^3]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, ch. 1, [para.](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-daaeef).
[^4]: *San Hyan Tattvajnana*, ch. 1, [para.](/works/unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd/01/translation/#p-d53c24).
[^5]: Cf. shared motifs of states (*jāgrat*, *tūrya*) and *ātmā*-*cetana* in both full chapters.
